quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Velocity and acceleration"?

options.text: G = 2∫₀¹ [x - L(x)]dx; ranges from 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (perfect inequality); measures income distribution
v(t) = s'(t) is the rate of position change; a(t) = v'(t) = s''(t) is the rate of velocity change
The derivative of the natural log; note that d/dx[ln|x|] = 1/x for x ≠ 0
The set of input values that make physical sense in the context; endpoints may produce absolute extrema

quiz_questions.explanation: "Velocity and acceleration" is defined as: v(t) = s'(t) is the rate of position change; a(t) = v'(t) = s''(t) is the rate of velocity change. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Instantaneous rate of change"?

options.text: The derivative f'(a) gives the exact rate of change of f at the specific point x = a, not an average over an interval
To find absolute extrema on [a,b]: 1) find critical points, 2) evaluate f at critical points and endpoints, 3) compare values
x-intercepts, y-intercept, local maxima and minima, inflection points, and behavior at asymptotes and endpoints
Left: sample at left endpoint; Right: sample at right endpoint; Midpoint: sample at center of each subinterval

quiz_questions.explanation: "Instantaneous rate of change" is defined as: The derivative f'(a) gives the exact rate of change of f at the specific point x = a, not an average over an interval. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Linear approximation"?

options.text: f' gives the topology (hills and valleys); f'' gives the shape (cupping up or down); together they determine the complete curve shape
Motion along a straight line (one dimension); described completely by position as a function of time s(t)
The + C in every indefinite integral accounts for the fact that F(x) + C has the same derivative for any constant C
f(x) ≈ f(a) + f'(a)(x-a); using the tangent line at x = a to estimate nearby function values

quiz_questions.explanation: "Linear approximation" is defined as: f(x) ≈ f(a) + f'(a)(x-a); using the tangent line at x = a to estimate nearby function values. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Marginal analysis"?

options.text: In economics: marginal cost C'(x), marginal revenue R'(x), and marginal profit P'(x) = R'(x) - C'(x) at production level x
An interior point c where f'(c) = 0 (horizontal tangent) or f'(c) does not exist (corner, cusp, or vertical tangent)
A point where the concavity of f changes; requires f''(x) = 0 or undefined AND a sign change in f''
= e^(f(x)) · f'(x); the most common chain rule application with exponentials

quiz_questions.explanation: "Marginal analysis" is defined as: In economics: marginal cost C'(x), marginal revenue R'(x), and marginal profit P'(x) = R'(x) - C'(x) at production level x. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Related rates setup"?

options.text: Find dy/dt given dx/dt (or vice versa) by differentiating an equation relating x and y with respect to time t
Choose h measuring depth down from the surface (h = 0 at surface); or set up y-axis and convert
When two quantities change with time, their derivatives are related through the chain rule: differentiate the equation relating them with respect to t
Divide the plate into thin horizontal strips of width dh; each strip has approximately constant pressure ρgh and area w(h)·dh

quiz_questions.explanation: "Related rates setup" is defined as: When two quantities change with time, their derivatives are related through the chain rule: differentiate the equation relating them with respect to t. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "v(t) = s'(t) is the rate of position change; a(t) = v'(t) = s''(t) is the rate of velocity change"?

options.text: Restricting domain
Velocity and acceleration
Derivative of tan x
Closed interval requirement

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Velocity and acceleration".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The derivative f'(a) gives the exact rate of change of f at the specific point x = a, not an average over an interval"?

options.text: One-sided limits
Linearity
Shadow problems
Instantaneous rate of change

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Instantaneous rate of change".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "f(x) ≈ f(a) + f'(a)(x-a); using the tangent line at x = a to estimate nearby function values"?

options.text: Notation
Linear approximation
Equilibrium solutions
When does the particle change direction?

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Linear approximation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "In economics: marginal cost C'(x), marginal revenue R'(x), and marginal profit P'(x) = R'(x) - C'(x) at production level x"?

options.text: Simplify before differentiating
Combining with u-substitution
Marginal analysis
When does the particle change direction?

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Marginal analysis".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "When two quantities change with time, their derivatives are related through the chain rule: differentiate the equation relating them with respect to t"?

options.text: Fundamental Theorem (Part 1)
Second derivative test
Related rates setup
d/dx[f(x)^g(x)]

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Related rates setup".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Applications of Derivatives is accurate?

options.text: Applications of Derivatives connects derivatives to real-world rate-of-change problems.

The method: (1) identify the quantity to optimize, (2) express it as a function of one variable using constraints, (3) find critical points where f'(x) = 0, (4) verify maxima/minima using the first or second derivative test, (5) check endpoints...
Implicit Differentiation finds dy/dx when y is not explicitly solved as a function of x.
The product rule extends to three or more factors: d/dx[fgh] = f'gh + fg'h + fgh'.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Applications of Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Applications of Derivatives?

options.text: Understanding growth rates is essential for comparing algorithms, modeling populations, and analyzing asymptotic behavior.
A slope field (direction field) displays short line segments at grid points (x,y), each with slope equal to f(x,y) from dy/dx = f(x,y).
If s(t) is position, then s'(t) = v(t) is velocity and s''(t) = v'(t) = a(t) is acceleration.
As n→∞, Riemann sums converge to the definite integral: ∫ₐᵇf(x)dx = lim(n→∞) Σf(xᵢ*)Δx.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Applications of Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Applications of Derivatives?

options.text: Riemann sums approximate this area using rectangles: left, right, and midpoint sums with n subintervals.
Continuous compound interest follows A = Pe^(rt).
The derivative represents instantaneous rate of change in any context: population growth rate, marginal cost, rate of temperature change, or rate of chemical reaction.
Antiderivatives: Integration Strategies develops a systematic approach to choosing the right technique.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Applications of Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Applications of Derivatives?

options.text: If f'(x) > 0 on an interval, then f is increasing on that interval; if f'(x) < 0, f is decreasing.
The key insight is that pressure increases with depth, so different parts of the plate experience different pressures.
The power rule: d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹ for any real n.
Linear approximation uses the tangent line to estimate function values near a known point: f(x) ≈ f(a) + f'(a)(x-a).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Applications of Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Applications of Derivatives?

options.text: Applications of Derivatives connects derivatives to real-world rate-of-change problems.
When k > 0: exponential growth (population, investment); when k < 0: exponential decay (radioactive decay, drug clearance).
Riemann sums approximate this area using rectangles: left, right, and midpoint sums with n subintervals.
This yields the derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions: d/dx[arcsin x] = 1/√(1-x²), d/dx[arccos x] = -1/√(1-x²), d/dx[arctan x] = 1/(1+x²).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Applications of Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Applications of Derivatives?

options.text: Speedometer readings as instantaneous velocity: the derivative of position with respect to time at each moment, computed electronically in modern vehicles
Oil spill spreading: a circular oil slick expanding on water has dA/dt = 2πr·dr/dt, used to estimate cleanup time and containment needs
Computing moments of inertia in physics: ∫x²·f(x)dx often requires integration by parts, essential for structural and mechanical engineering
Hydrology: ∫(inflow rate - outflow rate)dt gives the net change in reservoir water level over a drought period

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Applications of Derivatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Applications of Derivatives?

options.text: Computing moments of inertia in physics: ∫x²·f(x)dx often requires integration by parts, essential for structural and mechanical engineering
Medical imaging: CT scans reconstruct 3D images by computing rates of change of X-ray attenuation through tissue from multiple angles
Medical research: sketching dose-response curves to identify therapeutic windows (effective dose ranges) for drug development
Moore's Law: the roughly exponential growth of transistor density in microchips (doubling every ~2 years) eventually creating unforeseen limits in physics

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Applications of Derivatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Applications of Derivatives applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Velocity as the derivative of position: v(t) = s'(t) tells you the instantaneous speed and direction of a moving object at any moment
Financial markets: the 'Greeks' (delta, gamma, theta) in options pricing are derivatives that measure how option value changes with respect to stock price, rate of change, and time
Traffic analysis: given a graph of vehicle entry rate, computing total vehicles and identifying peak congestion periods
Submarine hull design: calculating pressure at operating depth determines hull thickness needed to prevent collapse

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Applications of Derivatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Applications of Derivatives?

options.text: Medical research: sketching dose-response curves to identify therapeutic windows (effective dose ranges) for drug development
Compound interest: computing d/dn[(1 + r/n)^(nt)] using logarithmic differentiation to analyze how compounding frequency affects growth
Speedometer readings as instantaneous velocity: the derivative of position with respect to time at each moment, computed electronically in modern vehicles
Structural engineering: the second derivative of a beam's deflection function indicates whether a critical point is a maximum deflection (sag) or minimum

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Applications of Derivatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Applications of Derivatives in practice?

options.text: Population growth modeling: d/dt[P(t)] where P involves power functions helps ecologists predict changes in wildlife populations
Medical imaging: CT scans reconstruct 3D images by computing rates of change of X-ray attenuation through tissue from multiple angles
Compound interest: computing d/dn[(1 + r/n)^(nt)] using logarithmic differentiation to analyze how compounding frequency affects growth
AP exam problems frequently ask students to apply MVT to piecewise data: 'Given the table, must there be a value c where f'(c) = 3?'

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Applications of Derivatives.
